synthesizes everything the book teaches into a coherent description of the master/work's method.

The user will give you the book-level distillation, the chapter summaries, and
the structured systems-draft JSON you just derived. Write the statement.

Structure:

  # <Work Title> — Statement of Outputs

  ## Overview
  (2-3 paragraphs on what the book is and what it teaches, citing the
  book-level distillation.)

  ## Systems
  For each system in the systems-draft, one ## section. For each:
    - one paragraph describing the system in plain language
    - the system's members listed
    - the traversal rules and modification rules listed with their
      engine_payload kinds shown in code formatting
    - citations back to the relevant chapter summaries that backed each
      claim (e.g. "(Chapter 2 summary; ch02.md quotes)")

  ## Pending Work
  List every `_pending:<kebab>` engine_payload.kind value with what it
  signals. If there are none, omit this section.

  ## Provenance Notes
  Brief: what's drawn from what. Note any chapters that didn't yield a
  system and why.

This is the human-facing artifact. Be precise, cite, and don't invent
content outside the summaries and quote files.

# Book-level distillation — improvisation-method-as-documented-2023

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

## Central Thesis

Moreno's NYU document records Peter Bernstein's teaching as the deliberate inversion of academic jazz pedagogy: improvisation is language use, not scale deployment, and every concept is anchored to a specific tune rather than to an abstract harmonic exercise. Chapter 1 frames the entire document as a record of oral apprenticeship rather than secondhand interpretation, and explicitly notes that Bernstein has no strict methodical structure and almost never explains a concept without anchoring it to a specific tune. Chapter 2 then names the pedagogical failure the method is designed to correct - the chord-scale drill that hands students scales over chord progressions without narrative or meaning - and replaces it with a jazz-as-conversation analogy in which the player needs vocabulary and syntax to assemble musical sentences that tell a story. Vocabulary is acquired the way children learn to speak (by listening and imitation), ideas/phrases (not scales) are the unit of practice, and improvisation is the recombination of known phrases under a syntax rather than real-time token generation. This linguistic frame is the spine on which every later chapter hangs.

## Pedagogical Arc

The document moves from epistemology to delivery in a deliberate sequence. Chapter 1 establishes source and method (oral tradition, living lineage, no formal system). Chapter 2 installs the language analogy as conceptual spine. Chapter 3 operationalizes that analogy into a curriculum for learning a tune: the standard is a poem, the melody is the story, and the comedian-and-jokes analogy (setups, narratives, punchlines learned from canonical examples) replaces the standard transcription-of-solos curriculum - tunes outrank solos, and the practice unit is the small phrase rather than the full transcribed chorus. Chapter 4 codifies melody primacy as the load-bearing rule: the first practice task on any tune is to play the melody as a monophonic instrument, and a player must not rush to 'step 12' (harmonic embellishment) before completing 'step 1' (melody mastery). Chapter 5 applies that hierarchy to comping via the dressing-the-notes metaphor, prescribing a layered build (root-3-7 first, extensions only after the relationship to the melody is heard) and a two-phase comping-construction procedure (design your own guide-tone line, then fill inner voices to dress those guide tones). Chapter 6 reframes harmony itself as the interplay of tension and resolution, with chords as tools rather than destinations. Chapter 7 builds the vocabulary-development apparatus - tritone ii-V substitution, three named variation methods, target-note practice, and a permissive chromatic stance. Chapter 8 names the central aesthetic - connected narrative outranks correctness-of-notes - and unifies soloing and comping under a single target-note system. Chapter 9 organizes the narrative and technical apparatus for solos and cadenzas (contrast, pacing, silence, rhymes, away-from-instrument practice, definitive endings). Chapter 10 closes with the aesthetic stance: technical prowess serves beauty and storytelling, and personal style emerges from the inherent beauty within one's limitations.

## Characteristic Bernstein Moves

A tight set of named operational moves recurs across the document. The melody-first procedure: play the tune as a monophonic instrument with attention to lyric syllables as a guide to articulation, then add conservative shell voicings, only then extensions and signature runs (Chapter 4). Target notes as the operational link: when the melody lands on a specific chord tone, the player notes it and lets it dictate voicing choice, illustrated by the Dm6-vs-Dm7 worked example where the melody's b7 overrides the default minor-tonic Dm6 (Chapter 4). Dressing the notes: comping's job is to give each melody note the right outfit so it feels important, with the B7#11 outfit example substituting a tension for a weakly doubled bass-note (Chapter 5). The two-phase comping construction: build your own guide-tone line per chord as a secondary melody, then fill inner voices to dress those guide tones (Chapter 5). Internal motion within a single chord: generate tension and resolution even when a single chord is sustained over multiple measures, and locate creative work in tension zones rather than at resolutions (Chapter 6). Creativity as recursive variation: ten ideas from one, then ten from each of those, formalized via three named variation methods - ear-led free variation that preserves overall structure, phrases built around one target note, and phrases built around two simultaneous target notes (Chapter 7). The party-for-the-target analogy: diatonic notes are family and chromatic notes are friends and neighbors, so any note can approach any target if it serves the line (Chapter 7). Comping voicings mirror solo targets: the top of Bernstein's comping voicings are drawn from the same melodic target notes he uses as solo landing points - one system covering two roles (Chapter 8). Practice away from the instrument by singing ideas over the form to establish a 'proper chain of command' where imagination leads the fingers (Chapter 9). Fingering serves articulation rather than convenience, with strong fingers (middle or ring) assigned to melodic target notes for tonal weight, and intervals felt by playing them on a single string with a subtle slide to emulate horn phrasing (Chapter 9). The one-shape, moving-hand move: maintain a single chord shape and shift the hand around the neck rather than reshaping fingers for each chord (Chapter 10). Record-and-play-over: record yourself comping for extended periods, then solo over the playback to evaluate the comping from the soloist's seat (Chapters 5 and 6).

## Sanctioned Exceptions and Contrarian Moves

The document is structured around several deliberate inversions of the standard jazz-school curriculum, and these read as sanctioned contrarian moves rather than exceptions to a rule. Tunes outrank solos as the source of concentrated vocabulary, so the practice unit is the composer's own phrases, not transcribed choruses (Chapter 3). Lead sheets and charts are excluded from the classroom - tunes are taught orally, section by section (Chapter 3). Chord-scale theory is rejected as the primary frame for improvisation (Chapter 2). Within the harmonic system, two characteristic contrarian moves surface explicitly: under-resolving the I chord, executed by substituting a V-derived I69 to keep tension unresolved over the tonic and avoiding the root of the I chord (Chapter 6); and the tritone-distance ii-V over a resolving dominant, with the explicit caveat that rhythmic placement of the substitution matters as much as its harmonic logic (Chapter 7). Technique is redefined away from speed and toward expressive range and the ability to adapt to other musicians' phrasing, particularly horn players' (Chapter 9). And the over-arching aesthetic exception: connected narrative outranks correctness-of-notes, meaning a player should prefer related ideas with purpose over notes that merely work over the chords (Chapter 8).

## Aesthetic Axioms

A small set of load-bearing aesthetic claims underwrites every practice rule in the document. Melody is primary and harmony exists to serve it; chords are placed in the spaces the melody allows, never the reverse, and the 'juggling on a unicycle' maxim warns against texture-overload at the cost of melodic feel (Chapter 4). Rhythm gives notes meaning - rhythm and the spaces between notes are what convert a string of pitches into a musical idea, and rhythm in comping is as load-bearing as voicing, including borrowing rhythmic motifs such as the intro of Parker's 'Star Eyes' (Chapters 4, 5, 6). Tensions are memorized by sensory flavor rather than symbol manipulation, and a single pitch carries different flavors under different chords (Chapters 4, 7). Contrast is the engine of narrative engagement - soft against loud, short against long, serious against lighthearted - with strategic pacing of subdivisions, silence as punctuation, and rhymes between ideas as the connective tissue across time (Chapter 9). Cadenzas demand definitive, unambiguous endings under the performer's control (parking-a-car, ending-a-conversation analogies; Chapter 9). Technique serves beauty and storytelling, and personal style emerges from discovering the inherent beauty within one's limitations rather than showcasing difficulty (Chapter 10). Improvisation is combinatorial exploration of a vast internalized library, with the practice route being to learn classic lines first and then progressively abstract them; Bernstein's relationship to the tradition is characterized as impressionistic abstraction of the great masters rather than literal reproduction, and the prescribed study covers the whole jazz tradition with primary emphasis on blues, swing, bebop, and hard bop (Chapter 10).

## The Method as a Unit

Taken together, the document records an integrated method with four interlocking commitments. First, terminology is fixed and load-bearing: jazz-as-language, tune-as-poem, melody-as-story, target notes, dressing the notes, guide-tone line, internal motion, party for the target, rhymes, contrast, one-shape moving-hand, chain of command (imagination leads fingers). Second, every concept is contextualized in a specific tune rather than presented as an abstract system - the methodological rule installed in Chapter 1 and re-asserted in Chapter 2. Third, melody is the organizing axis for everything: it dictates the first practice task (monophonic delivery), the second (basic chord relationship), the comping aesthetic (dressing the notes around guide tones), the solo content (lines that lead to the tune's own target notes), and the unified voicing-and-soloing system (top of comping voicings = solo landing points). Fourth, narrative outranks correctness - connected ideas with purpose, contrast, silence, and rhymes are the evaluative criteria, and the player is trained to internalize phrases by listening and imitation, to practice away from the instrument so imagination leads, and to read the tradition impressionistically rather than literally. The full curriculum sits inside an explicit anti-academic stance: there is no strict method, the document is the record of an oral apprenticeship, and the load-bearing artifacts are tunes, phrases, target notes, and the player's own ear - not scales, charts, or transcribed choruses.

# Chapter 1 — Introduction and Recurrent Themes

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

This chapter is introductory and biographical rather than instructional: Moreno frames the document as a record of roughly a year of lessons with Peter Bernstein at NYU, grounding its authority in direct apprenticeship rather than secondhand interpretation (Source and method). It positions Bernstein as a living repository of jazz tradition who simultaneously pushes the music forward (Bernstein as living tradition), motivating an oral-transmission style of pedagogy throughout the book. The chapter is explicit that Bernstein has no strict methodical structure and almost never explains a concept without anchoring it to a specific tune (No strict method) — the foundational constraint that shapes every later chapter. Moreno ties this anti-academic stance to Bernstein's own learning lineage of touring and playing with jazz elders rather than schooling (Learning by playing), establishing the load-bearing terms 'living tradition,' 'contextualized in tunes,' and 'oral, informal pedagogy' that the rest of the document depends on.

# Chapter 2 — Jazz as a Language and the Importance of Narrative

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

This chapter establishes the conceptual spine of Bernstein's method: improvisation is language use, not scale deployment. It reiterates the methodological rule that all ideas are presented inside specific tunes (Concepts live in tunes) and names the pedagogical failure it is designed to correct — the chord-scale drill that hands students scales over chord progressions without narrative or meaning (Critique of scale-chord pedagogy). Bernstein's central analogy is jazz-as-conversation: a player needs vocabulary and syntax to assemble musical sentences and paragraphs that tell a story (Jazz as conversation), and that vocabulary is acquired the way children learn to speak — by listening and imitation, not from books (How children learn language). The load-bearing operational moves are treating ideas/phrases (not scales) as the unit of practice (Ideas as words, scales as not-words), recombining known phrases under a syntax rather than generating tokens in real time (Generate vs. recombine), and, especially early on, learning situational selection — which phrase fits which situation — over exhaustive vocabulary recitation (Practical vocabulary use).

# Chapter 3 — How to Learn a Tune

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

This chapter teaches how Bernstein wants a student to approach learning a tune, extending the language analogy from chapter 2 into a concrete curriculum. Each standard is treated as a poem whose syntax, word-assembly, and rhymes the player is studying (Tune as poem), and within that poem the melody is the story itself while harmony and ornamentation are supporting customs, lights, and props (Melody as the story) — the hierarchy that the rest of the method depends on. Bernstein operationalizes narrative practice through a comedian-and-jokes analogy (setups, narratives, punchlines learned by studying canonical examples) (Comedian-and-jokes analogy) and inverts the standard transcription-of-solos curriculum: the composer's own ideas in the tune are the most concentrated vocabulary source, so tunes outrank solos (Tunes over solos) and the practice unit is the small phrase, not the full transcribed chorus (Skips transcription, learns phrases). The raw material to mine is named explicitly — rhythmic figures and core melody notes that become seeds for improvisation (Core elements of a tune) — and the chapter closes with the concrete classroom practice that enacts all of this: tunes are taught orally, section by section, with no lead sheets or charts (Oral-tradition lesson method).

# Chapter 4 — The Primacy of the Melody and Its Relation to Chords

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

This chapter codifies the load-bearing rule of the method: the melody is primary, and harmony exists to serve it. The order-of-operations is explicit — the very first practice task on any tune is to play the melody as a monophonic instrument and deliver the story, with attention to lyric syllables as a guide to articulation (Melody as monophonic delivery), and a player must not rush to 'step 12' (harmonic embellishment) before completing 'step 1' (melody mastery) (Step 1 before step 12). On solo guitar Bernstein enters with conservative shell voicings and only expands extensions and signature runs after the structure is established (Conservative chord entry); chords are placed in the spaces the melody allows, never the reverse (Never sacrifice melody phrasing), and the 'juggling on a unicycle' maxim warns against texture-overload at the cost of melodic feel (Juggling on a unicycle). The chapter introduces target notes as the operational link between melody and comping: when the melody lands on a specific chord tone the player notes it and lets it dictate voicing choice (Melody dictates chord choice), illustrated by the Dm6-vs-Dm7 worked example where the melody's b7 overrides the default minor-tonic Dm6 (Dm6 vs Dm7 from melody). Bernstein also reframes harmony pedagogy itself — tensions are memorized by sensory flavor rather than symbol manipulation (Chords as flavor/spice) — and insists melody and chords be learned together (Learn melody and chords together). The chapter closes by extending the language analogy to punctuation: rhythm and the spaces between notes are what convert a string of pitches into a musical idea (Rhythm gives notes meaning, Punctuation analogy).

# Chapter 5 — Comping and Supporting the Melody

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

This chapter applies the melody-first hierarchy to comping and introduces Bernstein's signature 'dressing the notes' concept. The concrete practice rule is to begin with root-3-7 voicings only, internalize the relationship between those basic chords and the melody, and add extensions only after that relationship is heard (Start with root, third, seventh) — a layered foundation-before-embellishment discipline (Foundation before embellishment). The load-bearing metaphor is dressing the notes: comping's job is to give each melody note the right outfit so it feels important (Dressing the notes), demonstrated by substituting a B7#11 voicing so the melody note becomes a tension rather than being weakly doubled by the bass (B7#11 outfit example). The chapter then specifies a two-phase comping-construction procedure: design your own guide-tone line per chord as a secondary melody (Build own guide tones), then fill the inner voices to dress those guide tones, recursively applying the same dressing-the-notes principle (Fill in around guide tones). Bernstein extends this from accompaniment into real-time reharmonization that anticipates the soloist's notes while preserving the song's harmonic narrative (Reharm to anticipate melody), and he prescribes a concrete practice routine — record yourself comping, then solo over the recording to feel whether the comping is interesting from the soloist's seat (Record and play over comping). Rhythm is elevated to equal status with voicing choice (Rhythm equal to chords), with a borrowed rhythmic cell from 'Star Eyes' shown becoming a developable motif (Rhythmic motif from Star Eyes), and the chapter closes on a responsive-comping ideal: the comper bends groove to the soloist's reality rather than the reverse (Flexible groove with soloist).

# Chapter 6 — Harmonic Movement, Tension and Substitutions

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

Chapter 6 lays out Bernstein's harmonic philosophy and the practice routines that operationalize it. The chapter's load-bearing claim is that jazz is fundamentally the interplay of tension and resolution—V's and I's with various flavors—and that chords are not destinations but tools for manipulating that tension (Jazz as tension and resolution, Chords as tools, not static entities). From this reframe flow several rules: generate internal tension/resolution even when a single chord is sustained over multiple measures (Internal motion within a single chord), and locate creative work in tension zones rather than at resolutions (Tension is where creativity lives). The chapter also gives a concrete practice routine—record yourself comping for extended periods, then solo over the playback to evaluate the comping from a soloist's seat (Record comping, then solo over it)—and insists that rhythm in comping is as load-bearing as voicing, including the practice of borrowing rhythmic motifs such as the intro of Parker's 'Star Eyes' (Rhythm equals chords in comping, Borrow rhythmic motifs for comping). Two characteristic moves surface explicitly: weaving passing chords and inner melodies between target sonorities, and avoiding the root of the I chord by substituting a V-derived I69 to keep tension unresolved over the tonic (Avoid the root of the I chord).

# Chapter 7 — Expanding the Vocabulary

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

Chapter 7 builds out Bernstein's vocabulary-development apparatus, starting from the forward-motion rule of under-resolving the I chord (Don't overemphasize the I chord) and a specific harmonic device—inserting a tritone-distance ii-V over a resolving dominant, with the caveat that rhythmic placement of the substitution matters as much as its harmonic logic (Tritone ii-V substitution, Rhythmic placement of substitutions). Voice leading is framed conceptually via Jim Hall: each string is a singer in a choir to be given a beautiful melodic line (Voice leading as choir writing). The chapter's core pedagogical claim defines creativity operationally as recursive variation—ten ideas from one, then ten from each of those (Creativity as variation generation)—and then enumerates three named variation methods: ear-led free variation that preserves the line's overall structure (Open approach to variation), phrases built around a chosen target note from the chord (Target notes around a chord tone), and then around two simultaneous target notes (Two-note target practice). The chapter introduces a permissive chromatic stance—any note can approach any target, dramatized by the 'party for the target' analogy where diatonic notes are family and chromatic notes are friends and neighbors (Chromatic notes around target, Party-for-the-target analogy)—and ties this to the observation that a single pitch carries different flavors under different chords (A note's flavor depends on harmony). It closes with the characteristic guitar-specific move of shape-based idea generation—playing what you see inside a fingerboard shape and then evaluating by ear, distinguishing intentional phrasing from 'pressing the buttons' (Shape-based idea generation, Phrasing vs pressing buttons).

# Chapter 8 — Melodic Target Notes and the Narrative of the Solo

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch08.md`._

Chapter 8 articulates the central aesthetic of Bernstein's improvisational method: connected narrative outranks correctness-of-notes, drawing a sharp line between ideas that relate to each other with purpose and merely playing notes that work over the chords (Narrative vs notes that work). The load-bearing concept is that the melody's own target notes—the tune's landing points—are the basis for improvisation, and the practice procedure is to identify those targets and then build melodic lines that lead to them, starting simply and growing in complexity (Melody target notes as basis, Lines leading to target notes). The chapter also unifies the soloing and comping systems by observing that the top voicings Bernstein chooses while comping are drawn from the same melodic target notes he uses as solo landing points (Comping voicings mirror solo targets). Two practical rules support internalization: choose fingerings that feel natural rather than ones that exhibit difficulty (Use natural fingerings to internalize), and ensure new vocabulary is flexible enough to appear unforced in the real-time conversation of a solo (Flexibility in delivery).

# Chapter 9 — Cadenza, Technique and Flexibility

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch09.md`._

Chapter 9 organizes Bernstein's narrative and technical apparatus for solos and cadenzas. Contrast—soft against loud, short against long, serious against lighthearted—is named as the engine of narrative engagement (Contrast as narrative tool), supported by a pacing rule that withholds dense subdivisions early and deploys them later to build anticipation (Strategic use of subdivisions), the linguistic role of silence as punctuation between ideas (Silence as punctuation), and 'rhymes' as a vocabulary for connecting motifs across time through call-and-response or parallel tension-resolution (Rhymes between ideas). To invert the dependency between imagination and fingers, Bernstein prescribes practicing away from the instrument by singing ideas over the song's form, establishing a 'proper chain of command' where imagination leads (Practice away from the instrument). Cadenzas are framed by two analogies—parking a car and ending a conversation—both insisting on definitive, unambiguous endings under the performer's control (Cadenza as parking a car, Cadenzas need clear endings). The chapter then redefines technique away from speed and toward expressive range and the ability to adapt to other musicians' phrasing, particularly horn players' (Technique redefined as expressive range, Imitate horn-player phrasing), with concrete characteristic moves: fingering is chosen to serve articulation rather than convenience, the strongest fingers (middle or ring) are assigned to melodic target notes for tonal weight, and intervals are felt by playing them on a single string with a subtle slide to emulate horn phrasing (Fingering serves articulation, Strong fingers on target notes, Single-string intervals and slides).

# Chapter 10 — Final Thoughts

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch10.md`._

Chapter 10 is a closing summary of Bernstein's aesthetic stance rather than new instructional material, organized around the maxim that technical prowess must serve beauty and storytelling, and that personal style emerges from discovering the inherent beauty within one's limitations rather than showcasing difficulty (Technique serves beauty and story, Beauty within limitations). The chapter frames Bernstein's path as deliberately narrow—a specific aesthetic cultivated over years and heavily influenced by traditional players (Narrow aesthetic path)—and models improvisation as combinatorial exploration of a vast internalized library, with the practice route being to learn classic lines first and then progressively abstract them into variations (Improvising from a vast library, Learn classic lines, then abstract). Bernstein's relationship to the tradition is characterized as impressionistic abstraction of the great masters rather than literal reproduction (Impressionist analogy), and he prescribes comprehensive study of the whole jazz tradition with primary emphasis on blues, swing, bebop, and hard bop (Study the whole jazz tradition). One concrete characteristic move surfaces: maintaining a single chord shape and shifting the hand around the neck rather than reshaping fingers for each chord (One shape, moving hand).

    {
      "id": "peter-bernstein:improvisation-method-as-documented-2023:melody-primary-improvisation",
      "name": "Melody-Primary Improvisation System",
      "summary": "Codifies the method's load-bearing pedagogical rule: melody is primary and harmony exists to serve it. The order-of-operations is explicit - the first practice task on any tune is to play the melody as a monophonic instrument, with attention to lyric syllables as a guide to articulation, before any harmonic embellishment. Target notes are the operational link between the melody and the harmony: when the melody lands on a specific chord tone, the player notes it and lets it dictate voicing choice. The 'juggling on a unicycle' maxim warns against texture-overload at the cost of melodic feel.",
      "members": [
        {
          "id": "monophonic-delivery",
          "name": "Monophonic Melody Delivery",
          "summary": "Play the tune as a single line with attention to lyric syllables."
        },
        {
          "id": "shell-voicing-entry",
          "name": "Conservative Shell Voicing",
          "summary": "Root-3-7 shell voicings as the chord layer added after melody mastery."
        },
        {
          "id": "extension-layer",
          "name": "Extension and Signature Run Layer",
          "summary": "Extensions and signature runs added only after the melody-chord relationship is heard."
        },
        {
          "id": "melody-target-note",
          "name": "Melody Target Note",
          "summary": "The specific chord tone the melody lands on; dictates voicing choice."
        }
      ],
      "traversal_rules": [
        {
          "id": "step-1-before-step-12",
          "name": "Step 1 Before Step 12",
          "summary": "Complete melody mastery before any harmonic embellishment; do not rush to step 12 before step 1.",

            "kind": "_pending:practice-ordering"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 4,
              "topic": "Step 1 before step 12",

            },
            {
              "chapter_n": 4,
              "topic": "Melody as monophonic delivery",
              "quote_excerpt": "play the melody as a monophonic instrument"
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "conservative-chord-entry",
          "name": "Enter With Conservative Shell Voicings",
          "summary": "On solo guitar, enter with conservative shell voicings and only then expand to extensions and signature runs.",

          },
          "references": [

          ]
        }
      ],
      "modification_rules": [

    {
      "id": "peter-bernstein:improvisation-method-as-documented-2023:comping-aesthetic",
      "name": "Comping Aesthetic System (Dressing the Notes)",
      "summary": "Applies the melody-first hierarchy to accompaniment via the signature 'dressing the notes' metaphor: comping's job is to give each melody note the right outfit so it feels important. The concrete practice rule is to begin with root-3-7 voicings only, internalize their relationship to the melody, and add extensions only after that relationship is heard. The chapter prescribes a two-phase comping-construction procedure - design a guide-tone line per chord as a secondary melody, then fill the inner voices to dress those guide tones. Tension and resolution are generated even inside a single sustained chord, and rhythm in comping is elevated to equal status with voicing choice.",
      "members": [
        {
          "id": "root-3-7-shell",
          "name": "Root-3-7 Shell Voicing",
          "summary": "The basic chord layer that establishes the melody-chord relationship before extensions are added."
        },
        {
          "id": "guide-tone-line",
          "name": "Guide-Tone Line",
          "summary": "A self-designed melodic line through each chord, functioning as a secondary melody under the tune."
        },
        {
          "id": "inner-voice-dressing",
          "name": "Inner-Voice Dressing",
          "summary": "Inner voices filled around the guide-tone line to dress those guide tones."
        },
        {
          "id": "extension-outfit",
          "name": "Extension-as-Outfit (e.g., B7#11)",
          "summary": "A tension chosen so the melody note becomes a color rather than a doubling."
        },
        {
          "id": "rhythmic-motif",
          "name": "Rhythmic Motif",
          "summary": "A borrowed or developed rhythmic cell (e.g., Star Eyes intro) used as a developable comping unit."
        }
      ],
      "traversal_rules": [
        {
          "id": "foundation-before-embellishment",
          "name": "Foundation Before Embellishment",

    {
      "id": "peter-bernstein:improvisation-method-as-documented-2023:vocabulary-development",
      "name": "Vocabulary Development System (Recursive Variation and Target-Note Practice)",
      "summary": "The apparatus by which a player expands the internalized library of phrases that improvisation recombines. Vocabulary is acquired the way children learn to speak - by listening and imitation - and creativity is defined operationally as recursive variation: ten ideas from one, then ten from each of those. The chapter enumerates three named variation methods (ear-led free variation, phrases built around a single target note, phrases around two simultaneous target notes) and pairs them with a permissive chromatic stance under the 'party for the target' analogy: diatonic notes are family, chromatic notes are friends and neighbors. The system closes with two practice meta-rules - practice away from the instrument so imagination leads the fingers, and learn classic lines first then progressively abstract them.",
      "members": [
        {
          "id": "free-variation",
          "name": "Ear-Led Free Variation",
          "summary": "Variation that preserves the line's overall structure while changing notes by ear."
        },
        {
          "id": "single-target-variation",
          "name": "Single Target-Note Variation",
          "summary": "Phrases built around a single chosen target note from the chord."
        },
        {
          "id": "two-target-variation",
          "name": "Two Simultaneous Target Notes",
          "summary": "Phrases built around two target notes at once."
        },
        {
          "id": "diatonic-family",
          "name": "Diatonic 'Family' Notes",
          "summary": "Diatonic notes around a target - the 'family' in the party-for-the-target analogy."
        },
        {
          "id": "chromatic-neighbors",
          "name": "Chromatic 'Friends and Neighbors'",
          "summary": "Chromatic notes that can approach any target if they serve the line."
        },
        {
          "id": "classic-line",
          "name": "Classic Line",
          "summary": "Canonical phrase learned by imitation, the starting unit for abstraction."
        }
      ],
      "traversal_rules": [
